preamble: The loss of palatalization on the alveolar trill — depalatalization of /rʲ/ to plain /r/ — is documented in Baltic and Slavic. In Standard Latvian, the phoneme /rʲ/ (written ŗ) gradually merged with /r/, with the letter ŗ officially removed from the alphabet in 1946. Across broad Slavic territory, Common Slavic *rʲ underwent dispalatalization between the 10th and 13th centuries, surviving only in specific phonological environments or dialects in some modern languages.
phoneticEffects: Depalatalization, Merger, Loss of Secondary Articulation

examples.note: Morning; the palatalized trill /rʲ/, written ŗ, merged with plain /r/ in Standard Latvian. The letter ŗ was officially removed from the alphabet by the Latvian SSR legislature on 5 June 1946.
To tear, pull; another instance of the ŗ→r merger following the 1946 orthographic reform.

Common Slavic *rʲ dispalatalized across broad Slavic territory in the 10th–13th centuries. Modern Standard Ukrainian lacks phonemic /rʲ/ entirely, in contrast to Russian where the contrast is preserved before vowels.

sources: Zalite, L. (2015). The Incidence and Evolution of Palatalized Consonants in Latvian. CUNY Graduate Center.
Lebedivna, O. (2022). Dispalatalization of Common Slavic *rʲ in Ukrainian: The case of family names in -uk- in the Hutsul parish registers. Journal Ukrainian Language, 2(82), 110.
Shevelov, G. Y. (1979). A Historical Phonology of the Ukrainian Language. C. Winter Universitätsverlag.
